learn.title: Levelised Cost of Energy (LCOE) Explained
learn.intro: LCOE is the gold standard metric for comparing electricity generation costs across different technologies. It helps policymakers and investors make informed decisions by calculating the average cost per MWh over a power plant's lifetime. Understanding LCOE is crucial for the energy transition as it reveals which renewable technologies are economically competitive against fossil fuels.
learn.c1_text: LCOE represents the total lifetime cost of building and operating an energy project divided by its total energy output. Think of it like calculating the true cost per mile of driving a car, including purchase price, fuel, maintenance, and depreciation. For 2023, solar PV achieved $26-44/MWh globally, making it highly competitive.
learn.c2_text: This metric enables apples-to-apples comparisons between solar, wind, hydro, and fossil fuel plants by accounting for capital costs, operations, fuel prices, and project lifespan. Advanced LCOE calculations include risk adjustments and external costs like carbon pricing. It's become the primary tool for governments setting renewable energy targets and subsidies.
learn.c3_text: India's solar LCOE dropped from over $100/MWh in 2012 to around $26/MWh by 2023, according to IRENA data. The country's solar auctions regularly achieve tariffs below $0.03/kWh. Wind power costs have similarly declined to $30-50/MWh range. These falling costs have enabled India to add 175+ GW of renewable capacity while keeping electricity prices stable.
learn.stat1: Solar LCOE fell from $100+ to $26/MWh in India (2012-2023)
learn.stat2: Global offshore wind LCOE ranges $70-110/MWh in 2023
learn.stat3: Solar PV costs declined 89% globally since 2010

learn.title: Pumped Hydro Storage Systems
learn.intro: Pumped hydro storage acts as the world's largest battery, storing excess energy by pumping water uphill during low demand periods and releasing it to generate power during peak times. This proven technology provides crucial grid stability and long-duration storage needed for high renewable energy penetration. With over 90% of global storage capacity, it remains essential for the energy transition.
learn.c1_text: The system uses two water reservoirs at different elevations. During surplus electricity periods, pumps move water from the lower to upper reservoir. When electricity is needed, water flows downhill through turbines to generate power. A typical plant can store 24+ hours of energy and deliver it within minutes, functioning like a giant gravitational battery.
learn.c2_text: Modern pumped hydro facilities offer 100-5000 MW capacity with 4-12 hour duration. They achieve round-trip efficiency of 70-85%, converting most stored energy back to electricity. Plants can start generating within 5-15 minutes of demand. The technology provides frequency regulation, voltage support, and black start capabilities, making it invaluable for grid stability.
learn.c3_text: India's first pumped hydro project at Renukut, Jharkhand, commissioned in 2023 with 1200 MW capacity. The country plans 13 more projects under development. India's 2024 renewable energy policy emphasizes pumped hydro for grid stability. The government targets 20 GW of pumped hydro storage by 2030, recognizing its role in managing 500+ GW renewable capacity.
learn.stat1: Pumped hydro provides 94% of global grid-scale storage capacity
learn.stat2: India's Renukut pumped hydro plant has 1200 MW capacity
learn.stat4: India targets 20 GW pumped hydro storage by 2030

learn.title: Direct Air Carbon Capture Technology
learn.intro: Direct Air Capture (DAC) removes CO2 directly from ambient air using chemical processes, then stores it underground or uses it for products like synthetic fuels. As global temperatures rise, DAC offers a pathway to achieve net-negative emissions. When powered by renewable energy, DAC becomes a crucial tool for combating climate change and meeting Paris Agreement targets.
learn.c1_text: DAC facilities contact large volumes of air through fans or natural diffusion. Chemical sorbents absorb CO2 molecules, then undergo regeneration using heat and pressure to concentrate pure CO2. Each ton of DAC removes approximately 1 ton of atmospheric CO2. Climeworks in Iceland currently stores CO2 underground, demonstrating commercial viability.
learn.c2_text: DAC requires substantial energy, typically 300-800 kWh per ton of CO2 captured. Solar and wind power provide ideal low-cost electricity for continuous operations. The process produces no air pollution but does require water for sorbent regeneration. Future plants will integrate directly with renewable installations to minimize transportation costs and maximize efficiency.
learn.c3_text: India's Solar Energy Corporation plans DAC pilot projects by 2025-26. The country's abundant solar potential makes renewable-powered DAC economically viable. India's National Hydrogen Mission includes DAC in its roadmap for achieving net-zero by 2070. Initial pilots will focus on industrial clusters in Gujarat and Rajasthan with existing CO2 pipeline infrastructure.
learn.stat1: DAC currently costs $600-1000 per ton of CO2 removed
learn.stat2: Climeworks' Orca plant in Iceland captures 4000 tons annually
learn.stat3: DAC requires 300-800 kWh energy per ton of CO2 captured
learn.stat4: India plans DAC pilot projects by 2025-26

learn.title: Offshore Wind Farm Development
learn.intro: Offshore wind farms harness stronger, more consistent winds located 12-50 miles offshore, generating significantly more electricity than land-based turbines. These massive projects require specialized vessels, installation techniques, and grid connections through subsea cables. With floating technology advancing, offshore wind could supply 11 TW globally by 2050, powering millions of homes while creating green jobs.
learn.c1_text: Offshore winds blow 20-40% faster and more consistently than onshore, enabling turbines to generate 40-60% more electricity annually. Offshore farms avoid land use conflicts and noise issues. Modern turbines reach 15 MW capacity with rotor diameters exceeding 200 meters. Europe leads with 65 GW installed, while the US aims for 30 GW by 2035 through ambitious lease auctions.
learn.c2_text: Projects require specialized installation vessels, weather windows, and subsea cable laying capabilities. Foundation types include monopiles, jackets, and floating platforms depending on water depth. Grid connections need high-voltage export cables and onshore substations. Projects typically take 3-5 years from planning to operation, requiring coordination between dozens of contractors and regulatory bodies.
learn.c3_text: India's 2024 offshore wind policy targets 100 GW capacity by 2030. The first major project, Suzlon's 150 MW Tamil Nadu site, began development. Government auctions awarded 7 GW of projects in 2023. Key states like Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, and Kerala have identified suitable sites. India aims to install 50,000+ offshore turbines, potentially becoming a global manufacturing hub.
learn.stat1: Offshore wind generates 40-60% more electricity than onshore
learn.stat2: Europe leads with 65 GW offshore wind capacity
learn.stat3: India targets 100 GW offshore wind by 2030
learn.stat4: Modern turbines reach 15 MW capacity with 200m rotor diameters

learn.title: Flow Batteries for Grid Storage
learn.intro: Flow batteries store energy in liquid electrolytes contained in external tanks, offering unlimited energy capacity and long-duration discharge capabilities. Unlike lithium-ion batteries, they can scale energy and power independently, making them ideal for grid-scale applications requiring 4-12 hour storage. Vanadium and iron-air systems are leading technologies, with costs declining rapidly as manufacturing scales up.
learn.c1_text: Flow batteries circulate liquid electrolytes through stacks where chemical reactions store and release energy. The electrolyte tanks determine energy capacity while the stack determines power output. A 10 MWh system might use a 5000-liter vanadium tank paired with a 1 MW stack. They can cycle indefinitely without degradation, offering 20+ year lifespans with minimal maintenance requirements.
learn.c2_text: Vanadium flow batteries use soluble vanadium ions for both electrodes, enabling elegant recharge without electrolyte replacement. They achieve 70-80% efficiency and cost $300-500/kWh. Iron-air batteries use abundant iron metal and air, costing potentially under $100/kWh but with 60-70% efficiency. Both offer 10+ hour duration and rapid response for grid services like frequency regulation.
learn.c3_text: India's 2024 Battery Storage Policy promotes domestic manufacturing of flow batteries. The government offers 50% capital subsidy for pilot projects. Tamil Nadu plans a 50 MWh vanadium system for grid stabilization. India's National Renewable Energy Laboratory collaborates with US DOE on iron-air technology transfer. Domestic production targets 10 GWh annual capacity by 2030.
learn.stat1: Flow batteries offer unlimited energy capacity scaling
learn.stat4: India targets 10 GWh domestic flow battery production by 2030

learn.title: Wind Turbine Electricity Generation
learn.intro: Wind turbines transform kinetic energy from moving air into electrical power through sophisticated aerodynamic design and generator technology. Modern three-blade turbines capture wind energy efficiently across varying speeds, converting rotational motion into electricity via generators. Understanding this process reveals how clean power is produced at scale, with a single turbine potentially powering hundreds of homes annually.
learn.c1_text: Wind turbines act like giant propellers with curved blades designed using aerospace principles. The airfoil-shaped blades experience different wind speeds on leading and trailing edges, creating lift that spins the rotor. Each revolution captures energy from a swept area often exceeding half a football field. Modern turbines achieve 45-55% efficiency, among the highest for any heat engine.
learn.c2_text: Most turbines use doubly-fed induction generators (DFIG) or full-scale converters. DFIG connects directly to low-speed wind through gearboxes, offering cost-effective variable speed operation. Full converters provide superior grid integration and control but cost more. Both technologies convert 15-20 RPM rotor speed to 1000+ RPM generator speed, producing electricity synchronized with grid frequency.
learn.c3_text: India ranks 5th globally with 43 GW wind capacity as of 2024. Tamil Nadu leads with 13 GW installed. Suzlon Energy and Vestas India manufacture turbines locally. The government's Surya Mitra portal helps identify wind sites. India aims for 60 GW wind power by 2027, with offshore wind adding another 100 GW. Wind farms generate 1.5 lakh crore units annually, meeting 10% of national electricity demand.
learn.stat2: India ranks 5th globally with 43 GW wind capacity
learn.stat3: A single turbine can power 600-900 homes annually
learn.stat4: India targets 60 GW wind power by 2027
